Time itself has no value.

What has value is the uncertainty of what can happen during that time. The more possibilities that remain, the more valuable that time becomes.

Understanding this can change how you look at theta decay and options overall.

Everyone is anticipating $GME to submit a Tender Offer directly to $EBAY shareholders. But what happens after that? Well, let’s say the offer drops in the morning right before the market opens. And then? Everything moves at once. IV immediately spikes for both stocks.

eBay moves higher toward the $125 headline price, but usually not all the way there, because the market immediately discounts deal risk, financing questions, and the uncertainty around how many holders will actually tender. 

GameStop trades the other way at first, as investors price in the cost, dilution, and integration risk of trying to absorb a much larger company using half cash and half volatile stock under a collar. Options flow explodes. Arbitrage funds start buying eBay and shorting GameStop, and intraday volatility in both names spikes as every new headline gets repriced through the spread.

From there, the offer has to stay open for at least 20 business days once it starts, and it can run longer if GameStop extends or amends it (happens quite often). That tender window becomes a concentrated volatility period. eBay’s stock trades up toward the offer but sits below it, while GameStop’s stock chops around as investors keep repricing dilution, financing risk, and where the collar will land. 

Because the offer is straight to shareholders, each holder decides individually whether to tender; the board will express their view, but it’s just another piece of information in the background. If GameStop drops their historic Q2 earnings results during this tender window, then GameStop can rally, improving collar terms and lowering expected dilution. 

Once the tender ends, the percentage of eBay that GameStop controls determines both if and how fast the tickers combine.

At or above 90%, GameStop can use a short form merger under Delaware law. That typically takes about one to three weeks after the tender closes, so the full journey from morning of offer drop to a single combined ticker is roughly five to seven weeks.

If they capture between 50-90%, GameStop controls eBay but needs a long form merger with a proxy, regulatory review, and a shareholder vote, stretching the path to something like three to five months end to end.

Below 50%, there is no merger. GameStop ends up as a large minority owner, eBay stays independent, and both tickers remain separate unless and until a new deal is launched later or maybe the strategy shifts for GameStop.

I don’t have a crystal ball, and this is just an outline of what to expect. So please don’t be angry or respond to this like I’m saying any of this is objective or fact.

Looks like London and Asia may push us down overnight.

If they do, I’m watching $735 first on $SPY.

That’s been the level buyers have defended and I want to see if they show up there again. If they do, I think we have a good chance of working our way back higher.

If $735 doesn’t hold, then my attention shifts to $730 with a possible overthrow to 728-729 to stop hunt the bulls and trap the bears on the #SP500. 

That’s where we’ve consistently seen put buying build throughout the week on the heat maps I’ve been sharing.

It also makes me wonder if today’s move into the close was more of a shakeout than the start of something bigger. That’s one of the reasons I took the short dated contracts off this afternoon.

If we get a flush toward $730, that’s probably where I’ll start looking for a dip buy with a move back toward $745-747.

The bigger picture hasn’t changed for me. My higher timeframe signals have remained bullish ever since $716. And I have remained and still remain in those October calls for the bigger picture. Just have trimmed and added along the way. Right now I’m not questioning the trend. I’m just trying to find the best location to enter the next short term trade.

See you all in the morning.

— TJ
